title: Should Your Museum Start a Podcast?
content:
Zoe Goldman never planned to become a podcast producer. Her background is in art history, not journalism or audio production, and she is the first to admit that her route into the medium was accidental. “I sort of fell into podcasting,” she says. Today she produces podcasts at the Getty in Los Angeles, where she has helped build a slate of shows ranging from intimate archival series to scripted historical fiction for children.

That accidental path has made her something of a kindred spirit for museum professionals across the sector, many of whom find themselves drawn to audio out of passion for their collections rather than any formal training. And when Goldman talks about podcasting, the conversation is less about microphones and editing suites than about a more fundamental question: should your museum be making a podcast at all?

creator: Manual Charr

title: How AI Turned a Museum’s Masterpieces into a 600-Year-Old Mystery
content:
When Argentina’s Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes opened its doors to an algorithm, a beer brand and a question nobody could quite answer, the result was the museum’s most popular interactive experience to date.

Stand in front of Pieter Brueghel’s The Peasant Wedding and you’ll see revellers, jugs, and tankards of something golden being passed around a crowded table. What you can’t know, what nobody can know, is exactly what they’re drinking. According to an algorithm built in Buenos Aires, however, there is a 78% probability it’s a Stella Artois.

That unlikely statistic is the basis of The Artois Probability, a collaboration between Stella Artois, creative agency GUT Buenos Aires and the Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes. The project asked whether data science could answer an art-historical question hiding in plain sight. The beer’s brewing heritage stretches back to Leuven in 1366, so how likely is it that the drinks in Europe’s great paintings, the Manets and Van Goghs and Brueghels, were actually an Artois?
snippet: When Argentina’s Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes opened its doors to an algorithm, a beer brand and a question nobody could quite answer, the result was the museum’s most popular interactive experience
creator: Manual Charr
